profile.summary: A Senior Customer Success Manager owns a portfolio of higher-value or more complex accounts, driving adoption, renewals, and expansion while protecting customer health. They coordinate with sales, support, product, and services, handle executive stakeholders, and often mentor newer CSMs informally. Their scope usually includes success planning, risk management, escalations, and turning customer signals into internal action.

known_challenges.text: Balancing reactive escalations with proactive account work across a busy book of business.
Managing renewal risk when product gaps, support issues, or weak implementation are outside their direct control.
Holding credibility with senior customer stakeholders while also pushing for adoption with day-to-day users.
Spotting churn signals early enough to change the outcome, not just report the risk.
Navigating tension with sales over account ownership, expansion timing, and customer expectations.

recommended_question_themes.why: Senior CSMs often carry complex accounts, so weak prioritisation quickly turns into missed risk signals and burnout.
This role is judged heavily on retention, and early discussion helps surface blockers before they become late-stage saves.
They need to influence customer leaders differently from end users, especially in strategic or at-risk accounts.
Customer outcomes often depend on product, support, and sales, so friction here directly affects results.
At senior level, they are usually expected to show stronger strategic thinking, not just excellent responsiveness.

terminology.meaning: A signal of account strength based on usage, sentiment, and risk.
Shared goals, actions, owners, and timelines for customer outcomes.
Quarterly business review with customer stakeholders.
How fully customers use the product in real work.
How quickly a customer reaches useful outcomes.
Contract continuation at the end of the term.
Likelihood that a customer reduces or leaves.
Growth through added seats, products, or usage.
Moving a customer to a higher-value purchase.
Selling an additional product to an existing customer.
Revenue kept and grown from existing customers.
Best estimate of renewals, risk, and growth.
Executive business review for senior customer leaders.
Shared checklist to drive a renewal or rollout forward.
Structured customer feedback brought into internal decisions.
Coordinating urgent issues across teams and stakeholders.
Who influences, uses, approves, or blocks progress.

listen_for: Whether they talk about customer outcomes or only activity and meetings.
How clearly they separate product issues, adoption issues, and relationship issues.
Whether risk language is early and specific or late and vague.
How they describe working with sales, support, and product during tough accounts.
Whether they mention executive sponsors, champions, and detractors distinctly.
avoid: Treating them like a support contact instead of a strategic account owner.
Reducing success to renewals alone and ignoring adoption quality and customer outcomes.
Assuming every expansion opportunity should be pushed immediately, regardless of trust or timing.
Blaming the CSM for churn drivers they surfaced early but could not directly fix.
